data.title: Amazon's stock is falling, as this trend from earnings has investors worried
data.description:
By Emily Bary

AWS didn't see the same growth acceleration as its cloud rivals, raising questions about the returns on Amazon's high AI spending

Amazon.com Inc. isn't seeing the same cloud momentum as its peers, and that's weighing heavily on its stock Friday.

Whereas Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) both showed major growth pickups in their cloud businesses, Amazon Web Services' results didn't pack quite the same punch. Amazon's (AMZN) second-quarter cloud revenue essentially matched expectations at $30.9 billion, up 17.5% from a year before. That was above the 16.9% clip seen in the first quarter, but the acceleration was marginal, in the view of a Roth Capital Partners analyst.

Microsoft Azure and Alphabet's Google Cloud both saw bigger growth pickups and big beats relative to expectations.

The comparative results raised questions on Wall Street about whether Amazon is doing enough to capitalize on artificial-intelligence demand for cloud services, even as it spends heavily on AI infrastructure. Amazon's stock is down nearly 7% in Friday morning action.

The company's quarterly cash capital expenditures were $31.4 billion in the quarter, up from $24.3 billion in the March quarter, and Amazon expects similar outlays for each of the remaining two quarters of the year. That suggests to Roth Capital Partners analyst Rohit Kulkarni that this year's capex could approach $120 billion, versus the prior suggestion of a $100 billion outlook.

Is the company getting enough return on that investment? That's an increasingly key issue.

"AWS remains the cloud GOAT" - meaning the greatest of all time - "but Azure and [Google Cloud] are catching up fueled by AI petrol while AWS' tank appears to be delivering less power," Bernstein's Mark Shmulik wrote in a note to clients. "This AI race is a marathon, and AWS has some catching up to do."

In his view, "management's commentary on competitive positioning and trajectory sounded less constructive than peers."

AWS was a hot topic on the call, with Amazon Chief Executive Andy Jassy telling investors that when you look at growth, "these are all really just moments in time."

"When we look at the results over the last number of quarters, there are some times where, as far as we can tell, we're growing faster than others and sometimes others are growing faster than us," he said. "But it's still, if you look at second-place player ... it's still a pretty significant ... market-segment leadership position that we have."

Amazon's security offerings in the cloud seem to be resonating on a relative basis, he added.

MoffettNathanson's Michael Morton wrote that "the entirety of our discussions on Amazon have focused on the need for AWS to reaccelerate, and the debate if the company is falling behind in AI while also beingunderserved [graphics processing units]" by Nvidia (NVDA).

"For what it's worth, in our conversation with the company, they do not believe they are being deprioritized versus the competition with their allocation of GPU chips, an encouraging data point," Morton wrote.

UBS's Stephen Ju said that "part of the pullback in [Amazon] shares was due to the lack of a more explicit confirmation of potential revenue acceleration, even as [capex] budgets for AWS?work higher."

But he thought the post-earnings selloff in Amazon's stock seemed excessive as, by his math, "the difference between rounding up to reach the high end of investor expectation of 18% for AWS [year-over-year] growth vs rounding down to just 17% and therefore a 'miss' is some $10 [million] in revenue," which is fairly negligible for a company doing about $31 billion in revenue a quarter.

Nonetheless, the cloud performance and commentary overshadowed upbeat results in the cloud.

"If Amazon's retail business was a standalone entity, it would be trading dramatically higher following the near perfect results that lead us to raise our retail profitability outlook materially, enough to overpower our negative revisions to the AWS [operating-income] outlook," MoffettNathanson's Morton wrote. "Unfortunately, as we all know, the success of the retail business is not what's going to matter in the near term for Amazon's stock price."

See more: Apple's earnings were good, but the initial stock move implies Wall Street is wary

-Emily Bary

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  08-01-25 1001ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: 'I have never been asked for money before': My friend wants to borrow $1,600 to pay her rent. Do I say yes?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I have heard mixed reviews on lending people money'

Dear Quentin,

A friend asked me to loan her $1,600 to pay her rent. She is in a tough spot. She will get the funds from her account in 30 days to pay me back. I have never been asked for money before and I have heard mixed reviews on lending people money. If I say yes, is there some sort of documentation I can write up that states the terms?

Trying to be a Good Friend

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

Don't miss: My father has dementia and 'forgave' my brother's $200,000 house loan. The nursing-home notary said he was of sound mind. What can we do?

Dear Trying,

It's OK to say no.

First, if your friend asks you for money for rent, she will ask again. If she finds herself in debt, and knows she has exhausted your pocketbook or patience, she will ask another friend or acquaintance to help her out. That's not a personal judgement on your friend, per se, as I don't know anything about her. It's based on what you told me about her: she can't pay her rent, she asked a friend for money or she may or may not be able to pay it back.

On that latter issue, few people ask for money without a promise to repay it. You are not going to hand over $1,600 to a person who says, "And in 30 days, I expect to be in an even worse financial situation than I am today, so I won't pay you back." If she is in a position now where the cupboard is bare and the rent is due, as horrible a position as that is, she is more likely than the average person who has not borrowed money from a friend to be in that position again.

You're not a bad person if you say no, and it doesn't make you a bad friend.

Third, here's another tip to put in your pocket: You may not be the first person your friend has asked and, if you say no, you probably won't be the last. So before you answer, know that it's OK to say no. It's OK to say, "I can't spare it." Or better yet, "I don't have it." You're not a bad person if you say no, and it doesn't make you a bad friend. It makes you someone who needs to set boundaries and put your own financial stability first.

Finally, it's not only a privilege for you to be asked such a question, it's also a burden. Of all the people in your friend's life who she could have asked, she chose you. Why? Does she view you as a kind, compassionate and generous person? And/or does she see you as a soft touch, someone who will be guilted into handing over the dough? Both things can be true. You didn't say "yes" right away due to the awkwardness of the moment. And you didn't say "no" right away. That's OK too.

There is, on occasion, a high price to pay for being a soft touch. Ask this man who wrote to me to say that, bit by bit, his friends and family borrowed $120,000, none of which he saw again. Why? Because every person who borrowed money had a problem or, perhaps, was not to blame for the pickle in which they found themselves. "Blame," of course, is a useless, destructive word. A better one is accountability. The borrowers were not accountable.

Related: My coworker found out I inherited money - and harassed me to give him a loan

"They all had the following in common," he wrote. "These 'friends' were users and abusers, from people who had a dependency on drugs and alcohol and refused to seek help to nonpayers or "one-way" partners using me for my expertise. It was "Adios, amigos!" This was costly and time-consuming. I paid legal fees, wrote off money owed to me, etc. Of the original 10 I had earmarked for removal from my life, I'm still in contact with two. These are siblings."

It's not your responsibility to pay your friend's rent, so don't feel under pressure to give her the money if you are not comfortable doing so. It's her responsibility to pay her rent and it's your responsibility to pay your own rent or mortgage. As I told this woman who wondered whether she should pay $800 a month towards her husband's $67,000 student debt, it's your friend's job to complete her own proverbial paper route and it's your job to complete yours.

If you do say yes, do so with the knowledge that you're not being guilt tripped.

But while we're here. If you do say yes, do so with 100% confidence that you are doing so without being guilt tripped; only you know the cut of this friend's jib: A rock-solid loan agreement should have all of the terms and interest rates, how the loan will be repaid (in installments or a lump sum) and, as you indicate, by what date. It should also be witnessed and notarized, which would help if a legal dispute arises.

And now the kicker: Only give it if you can afford to never see that money again. The Internal Revenue Service puts a limit on such capital losses of $3,000 a year. "The debt must have been a bona fide loan - you gave the money with every expectation of being repaid," says TurboTax. "If you charged interest, and the borrower signed a promissory note, this provides a good indication that you expected to get your money back."

You can be a good friend and ask your bestie for a loan of $1,600. You can be a good friend and say no to a bestie who asks you for $1,600. Friendship should not be quid pro quo, but once you loan money, know that the balance could shift, and your friendship may never be the same again. Let's say you say no; it doesn't mean you're saying no to the friendship. And if your friend frames it as such, and makes the loan conditional on that basis?

In that case, there wasn't much of a friendship there to begin with.

Related: 'How do I shield my retirement savings?' I'm worried about Trump's trade war and Fed's willingness to cut interest rates

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

My therapist said he was in-network. He's not. Am I obliged to pay out-of-network rates for all prior sessions?

Can my husband contest his late brother's $600K will? He experienced oxygen deprivation due to COPD before he died.

I have early Alzheimer's and my husband has stage 4 kidney disease. We just inherited $50K. How can this help us?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  08-01-25 1003ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: BREAKING: Fluor Corp. Shares Down Over 20%; Investors Should Contact Block & Leviton to Potentially Recover Losses
data.description:
BOSTON, Aug. 01, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Block & Leviton LLP is investigating Fluor Corp. (NYSE: FLR) for potential securities law violations. Investors who have lost money in their Fluor Corp. investment should contact the firm to learn more about how they might recover those losses. For more details, visit https://blockleviton.com/cases/flr.

What is this all about?

Shares of Fluor Corp. (NYSE: FLR) lost over 20% of their value in pre-market trading on August 1, 2025, after the company reported second quarter results and lowered its full-year earnings guidance. The company cited growing costs on three infrastructure projects due to subcontractor design errors, schedule delays, and price escalation, along with reduced capital spending from clients. These issues were not previously disclosed, despite prior reaffirmations of full-year guidance.

Who is eligible?

Anyone who purchased Fluor Corp. common stock and has seen their shares fall may be eligible, whether or not they have sold their investment. Investors should contact Block & Leviton to learn more.

What is Block & Leviton doing?

Block & Leviton is investigating whether the Company committed securities law violations and may file an action to attempt to recover losses on behalf of investors who have lost money.

What should you do next?

If you've lost money on your investment, you should contact Block & Leviton to learn more via our case website, by email at shareholders@blockleviton.com, or by phone at (888) 256-2510.

Whistleblower?

If you have non-public information about Fluor Corp., you should consider assisting in our investigation or working with our attorneys to file a report with the Securities Exchange Commission under their whistleblower program. Whistleblowers who provide original information to the SEC may receive rewards of up to 30% of any successful recovery. For more information, contact Block & Leviton at shareholders@blockleviton.com or by phone at (888) 256-2510.

Why should you contact Block & Leviton?

Block & Leviton is widely regarded as one of the leading securities class action firms in the country. Our attorneys have recovered billions of dollars for defrauded investors and are dedicated to obtaining significant recoveries on behalf of our clients through active litigation in the federal courts across the country. Many of the nation's top institutional investors hire us to represent their interests. You can learn more about us at our website www.blockleviton.com, call (888) 256-2510 or email shareholders@blockleviton.com with any questions.

This notice may constitute attorney advertising.

CONTACT:
BLOCK & LEVITON LLP
260 Franklin St., Suite 1860
Boston, MA 02110
Phone: (888) 256-2510
Email: shareholders@blockleviton.com

data.title: Trump's tariffs are a no-win for Americans. Here's what needs to happen now.
data.description:
By David Blond

The world will move on without the U.S. at the center, and Americans' living standards will suffer

President Donald Trump's "liberation day" tariffs - part 2 - are slated to become effective on Aug. 1. Meanwhile, Trump is getting trade deals from Europe, Japan and the U.K., marked by promises of new investment in U.S. manufacturing, and purchases of American products.

Ignore the window dressing of these deals. There is no yardstick by which these commitments can be measured, so they are just nice words to pacify the president.

Nothing about the Trump plan for America's future as a major economic power makes any sense. I wonder if the officials who surround him fully understand the damage that is being done. Tariffs will not solve anything, and attacks on American science, universities and programs for supporting critical infrastructure and industries are damaging to America's economic strength going forward. Rejection of wind and solar power and a return to coal amounts to government malpractice.

You can almost see the worry and fear that Trump and his trade team are showing as they attempt to make more deals. Trump has staked his presidency on the idea that tariffs are paid by foreign sellers and not by American consumers. Backing away is not in the president's chemistry, so we may see more threats.

Wall Street blindly sees tariffs as no big deal and doesn't appear concerned about the added expenses for companies buying steel and aluminum, as well as higher prices for U.S. consumers. But there is nothing positive about these trade deals, and American companies have much to lose. America's trading partners can find better options than a U.S. market with higher prices and an economy shifting from boom to slow growth or bust from the economic malpractice of the Trump team.

Looking for any silver lining is wishful thinking by Wall Street. The truth is that the U.S. market is significant, but not that important if the global trade flows begin to rebalance with less sold across the Atlantic and Pacific and more between countries that are still willing to adhere to the hard-won tariff regimes that had served the global trading system so well for so many years - since the start of the Bretton Woods agreement that set the stage for today's interconnected world.

What needs to happen now

It's a long shot, but backing off this ledge is the only rational choice. If Trump decides to retreat from his trade positions, the administration would also need to call off the ICE raids and offer immigrants, a key part of America's labor force, a path to citizenship or at least permanent work permits while also securing the border. Trump would need to stop attacking the jewels of American education or the funding for needed research that strengthens America's economy. He would recognize that international trade has attracted investment to liquid, secure U.S. financial markets and given Americans the high standard of living they've enjoyed.

In truth, Trump's goal is worthy - rebuild American manufacturing and promote exports, and reduce the U.S. trade deficit with the world. But he is going about it in dangerous ways with tariffs and dealmaking. American companies don't want to export more to Europe from U.S. factories; they want to sell more in Europe from their own company subsidiaries or distributors. Deals only work if there is a specific barrier that is stopping trade, not by reducing a tariff that was already low. Better for the U.S. to specialize in the new, higher-value industries that benefit from America's deep financial resources and scientific infrastructure.

Trump wants to burn coal, but power companies know the truth. They are investing instead in wind, solar and nuclear power - not in bringing back a dying and polluting source of energy. Instead, convert mothballed coal-fired plants to green energy from small nuclear reactors, using existing turbines and cooling towers to power new data centers. Clean energy from alternatives is the future, but the Trump administration and its allies in Congress want to push the U.S. back to polluted cities and rising temperatures and seas. If they think the American people aren't taking notes, just wait until the 2026 election.

David Blond is an international economist and consultant. He has been quantifying and modeling the global system of trade and industrial development for more than 50 years.

More: Trump's tariffs are back in court: What to know.

Also read: Inflation posts biggest rise in four months, PCE shows. Tariffs may be filtering into prices.

-David Blond

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  08-01-25 0952ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Student-loan interest restarts today for millions. This is what you can do.
data.description:
By Jillian Berman

Borrowers enrolled in the SAVE plan will see their balances start to tick up

For the past few weeks, Cara Burgess has been panicking about her student loans.

Burgess, 42, is on the SAVE plan, a repayment program launched by the Biden administration that's been tied up in court for roughly the past year. After a federal court blocked the Education Department from moving forward with SAVE last summer, the Biden administration put borrowers into a forbearance that paused their payments and interest. But interest is set to resume today and if borrowers don't make those payments, their balances will start to tick up.

The announcement from the Trump administration in early July that interest would be restarting on Aug. 1 prompted Burgess to research her options outside of SAVE. She found that the cheapest plan would run her about $500 a month.

"That's totally unaffordable, like not possible," said Burgess, who said she and her husband and two children already avoid any extras like dining out and vacations, and still sometimes struggle to replace clothes the kids have outgrown. Even though the interest is restarting, Burgess said she plans to ride out the SAVE forbearance as long as possible and hopefully file her taxes separately from her husband next year so she can have a lower payment.

"I just feel really stuck," she said.

Burgess isn't alone in her frustration, experts say. She's one of roughly 7.7 million borrowers enrolled in SAVE who are trying to determine their best options. They're deciding whether to stay on SAVE or find a different plan amid an application backlog for income-driven repayment, the suite of plans that are typically the most affordable for borrowers.

They're also making this decision - and trying to plan their financial future - amid news that the student-loan system is slated to undergo a fundamental reshaping over the next few years thanks to the Republican tax and spending bill passed in July. The Education Department has temporarily paused forgiveness on one of the only income-driven repayment options that will remain available once the law is implemented, called income-based repayment - stoking confusion, experts say.

Meanwhile, the Trump administration slashed the Department of Education's staff in half and millions of borrowers are expected to default on their loans in the coming months, putting them at risk of experiencing some of the harshest consequences of the system, like wage garnishment, for the first time in five years.

"Folks who are on SAVE, they're the ones actively reaching out for help because they're a little bit confused," said Carolina Rodriguez, the director of the Education Debt Consumer Assistance Program, which works with student-loan borrowers in New York state. "There is a lot of confusion about repayment-plan options for people."

Here's what borrowers in SAVE need to know right now

Interest is resuming for SAVE borrowers starting on Aug. 1. That means if you're on SAVE you'll start to see your balance grow if you don't make payments. But payments are still paused, meaning you don't have to make a payment.

The Trump administration has said that the resumption in interest is necessary to comply with the court injunction blocking SAVE. Still, others, including prominent Democratic lawmakers, say the Education Department has the authority to pause interest.

In announcing the resumption of interest, Secretary of Education Linda McMahon encouraged borrowers to "quickly transition" to a different payment plan. "Borrowers in SAVE cannot access important loan benefits and cannot make progress toward loan discharge programs authorized by Congress," she said.

If you're enrolled in SAVE, but not pursuing forgiveness:

Despite that advice, some borrowers may consider riding out the payment pause, said Betsy Mayotte,the president of the Institute for Student Loan Advisors, a nonprofit that works with borrowers. Mayotte specifically points to some borrowers who who aren't pursuing debt relief through Public Service Loan Forgiveness, or income-driven repayment, a suite of options that allows borrowers to pay their debt as a percentage of their income and have the remainder canceled.

Those borrowers may want to stay on the forbearance, perhaps because they can't afford other repayment options right now or because they're focused on paying down other debt, Mayotte said.

Borrowers may also want to use the forbearance period to target certain loans for aggressive repayment. Borrowers staying on the forbearance can still put money toward the debt to pay down the interest.

"There's 1,000 different scenarios, so there's no one answer to this," she said.

For borrowers who are pursuing forgiveness:

For borrowers pursuing forgiveness programs, particularly under income-driven repayment, switching out of SAVE and into a different income-driven plan probably makes the most sense, Mayotte said.

That way they can get onto a plan where monthly payments count toward debt relief.

Borrowers applying for income-driven repayment may face a wait while their application is processed. As of mid-July there was a backlog of roughly 1.5 million applications.

"Some people, especially people submitting new applications, are getting processed quickly," Mayotte said. "There's other people still hanging out in the queue and I can't really put my finger on what's what and where."

One thing is for sure: If you previously applied for income-driven repayment and selected either the SAVE plan or asked your servicer to put you in the plan with the lowest-monthly payment, your application will be rejected sometime in the coming weeks, said Scott Buchanan, the executive director of the Student Loan Servicing Alliance, a servicer trade group.

"You can wait," to get that notice before you reapply, "but odds are if you selected SAVE or selected lowest-monthly payment you're going to get told you need to reapply anyway," he said.

'The biggest issue is the uncertainty'

For borrowers like Burgess, sorting through the options over the past few weeks is just one of the many times in recent years that she's had to devote energy to keeping up with changes to her student loans.

About a year ago, Burgess, who lives in Rhode Island, was feeling more hopeful about her debt. She'd heard the Biden administration was going to review borrowers' accounts and adjust them to ensure that borrowers had the correct number of payments counted toward forgiveness. Just days before Biden left office, her account showed that she had 249 out of 250 payments needed to qualify for debt relief under an income-driven plan.

But the court challenges to SAVE and the backlog of income-driven repayment applications have left her stuck at that number. Now, Burgess is unsure of what will come next for her loans. She's done her best to manage them for years even amid slow periods of work while she was a caretaker for one of her children who is deaf and visually impaired.

"For me right now, the biggest issue is the uncertainty," she said. "It's a ridiculous amount of work for the people who owe to figure out 'what plan should I be on? What's happening with the plan?'"

"There's a lot of people online in the internet world looking for answers," Burgess, who regularly talks with her servicer and checks Reddit and Facebook forums related to student debt, added.

-Jillian Berman

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  08-01-25 0927ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Pheton Holdings Ltd Issues Statements Addressing Recent Market Activity and Misleading Rumors
data.description:
BEIJING, China, Aug. 01, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Pheton Holdings Ltd (the “Company,” “we,” “our” or “Pheton”), a healthcare solution provider specializing in treatment planning systems for brachytherapy and other related products and services, today issued the following statements in response to recent market volatility and a third-party publication that has raised questions and concerns among shareholders.

Over the past few days, shares of Pheton have experienced an extraordinary and unexpected decline in its share price, which the Company believes may have been triggered by a speculative article published by The Bear Cave on July 29, 2025. The article asserts that Pheton’s share price may have been influenced by false rumors of a potential acquisition by Gilead Sciences, Inc. (“Gilead”).

Management's Position and Clarification

Pheton’s management team unequivocally and categorically denies any involvement in, or knowledge of, any form of stock price manipulation. At no point has the Company participated in, initiated, or sanctioned any rumor, communication, or activity regarding an acquisition by Gilead, or any other party. Pheton has had no contact with Gilead, and any statements or reports suggesting otherwise were and are entirely false and fabricated.

Additionally, Pheton plans to engage with its market makers, Nasdaq, and relevant regulatory bodies for the purpose of holding responsible parties to the scheme accountable.

Protecting Shareholder Interests

We recognize the concern this incident has caused among our investors, and we are monitoring internal controls and communications to ensure that any further misinformation can be swiftly and effectively countered.

Commitment to Transparency and Growth

Pheton is committed to upholding the highest standards of corporate governance, transparency, and accountability. As always, we remain focused on creating long-term shareholder value through carefully considered growth opportunities that align with our mission and strengthen our market position.

Pheton has built its foundation on trust, innovation, and value creation and remains confident in the strength of its underlying business, long-term strategy, and the dedication of its team.

A Word to Our Shareholders

We deeply value the trust our shareholders place in us. We will continue to keep the investor community informed with accurate and timely disclosures. Our leadership remains focused on operational execution and strengthening the integrity of our public market presence.

About Pheton Holdings Ltd

Founded in 1998, Pheton Holdings Ltd, through its wholly owned operating subsidiary, Beijing Feitian Zhaoye Technology Co., Ltd. (“Beijing Feitian”), focuses on healthcare solutions for brachytherapy, a targeted radiation therapy used in cancer treatment. Its lead product, Beijing Feitian’s Treatment Planning System, helps ensure safe and effective brachytherapy using radioactive sources inside the patient to kill cancer cells and shrink tumors. Pheton is committed to leveraging its products and services to establish a potential new standard of care across multiple malignant tumor applications. For more information, please visit: http://www.ftzy.com.cn/ir/

Forward-Looking Statements

Certain statements in this announcement are forward-looking statements. These forward-looking statements involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties and are based on the Company’s current expectations and projections about future events that the Company believes may affect its financial condition, results of operations, business strategy and financial needs. Investors can identify these forward-looking statements by words or phrases such as “approximates,” “believes,” “hopes,” “expects,” “anticipates,” “estimates,” “projects,” “intends,” “plans,” “will,” “would,” “should,” “could,” “may” or other similar expressions. The Company undertakes no obligation to update or revise publicly any forward-looking statements to reflect subsequent occurring events or circumstances, or changes in its expectations, except as may be required by law. Although the Company believes that the expectations expressed in these forward-looking statements are reasonable, it cannot assure you that such expectations will turn out to be correct, and the Company cautions investors that actual results may differ materially from the anticipated results and encourages investors to review other factors that may affect its future results in the Company's filings with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

For investor and media inquiries, please contact:

Pheton Holdings Ltd
Investor Relations
Email: ir@ftzy.com.cn

Jackson Lin
Lambert by LLYC
Tel: +1 (646) 717-4593
Email: jian.lin@llyc.global

###

data.title: Bowman, Waller Explain Their Fed Dissents
data.description:
By Matt Grossman 




  Federal Reserve governors Michelle Bowman and Christopher Waller favored an interest-rate cut this week because they have grown more concerned about the labor market, and thought their colleagues were being too cautious about inflation. 



  Bowman and Waller explained their dissents against the Fed's hold-steady move in separate statements Friday morning. 



  The Trump appointees' votes in favor of rate cuts marked the first time that two Fed governors dissented against the Fed's majority decision in 32 years. 



  "I believe that the wait and see approach is overly cautious," Waller said. 



  He reiterated a belief he has emphasized consistently this year: that tariffs will cause one-time price increases but not sustained inflation, an adjustment to prices that the Fed shouldn't lean against. 



  Bowman said the Fed's hesitance to cut rates could drag on economic growth. 



  "I see the risk that a delay in taking action could result in a deterioration in the labor market," she said. 



  Their statements were posted by the Fed this morning before the publication of the Labor Department's July jobs report, which showed a cooling in U.S. job creation. 





  Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  August 01, 2025 09:19 ET (13:19 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: I'm in my 60s. My salary is not keeping up with inflation. Do I jump ship?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'If my salary had kept pace with inflation, I would be earning an additional $17,000 a year'

Dear Quentin,

Every year, my overall annual performance rating has been "meets expectations," with some factors at "exceeds expectations." I'm in my 60s. During the first few years, my salary increases were in line, or close to in line, with the annual inflation rate. I've asked my managers for a promotion, but I keep getting the same song and dance ("there's no headcount for a promotion" and "someone at that level would need to leave the team, and you would have to apply").

Ten years ago, I changed employers due to a corporate downsizing. I've been with the same employer since then, working in the same team, same position, although the managers have changed several times and the work - which is technical in nature - has progressively become more complex, challenging and demanding and requires a significant degree of specialized subject-matter expertise, which is not widely transferable to other employers.

Recognized at work

I've always met the challenges of my work, including being nominated for and presented with awards for the impact I have made. I have received nice recognition, such as: "Thank you for your ownership, dedication and collaboration on this critical client quality escalation. Because of your knowledge, efforts and ownership we were able to provide the customer the root cause and a corrective action plan."

Last night, I took my annual salary from 10 years ago and applied it against a consumer-price index calculator for the same period. If my salary had kept pace with inflation, I would be earning an additional $17,000 a year. My purchasing power is 88% of what it was 10 years ago. Is it unreasonable to think that if an employee is meeting or exceeding expectations at a company that is publicly bragging about increasing net revenues, then the compensation increases should match inflation?

My future pension would be $800 a month if I work to age 70 with 17 years at this company. My less-than-inflationary salary increases will negatively affect the calculation of my Social Security benefit when I eventually retire. If I was earning $17,000 a year more, I'd be contributing at least another $1,000 annually to my 401(k), and my employer would be matching that with at least $500 every year.

Do I jump ship? What do you think?

Feeling Let Down at Work

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

Related: My $250,000 term life policy costs $2,000 a month. I'm 80. Is it time to ditch it?

Dear Feeling,

Beware of too many "ifs." Your finances are what they are, and it's best to plan your retirement with those figures front and center.

The jobs market is softening. Given that you are edging closer to retirement, it may be better to stick with the devil you know. There are no perfect employers, just like there are no perfect employees. Your retirement calculations would be more disappointing if you jumped ship without another job. If you do move, the policy for staff reductions can be first in, first out. The economy added just 73,000 new jobs in July and the unemployment rate rose to 4.2%, up from 4.1% in June. (Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had forecast 100,000 new jobs in July.)

When it comes to pay, what goes up rarely comes down, so most employers bestow pay hikes on employees carefully, if not always fairly. The average pay raise is 3% to 5%, accounting for inflation and merit increases. Companies are under no legal mandate to give employees salary hikes in line with inflation, unless specified in a union agreement. Without such an agreement, the prospects of you getting a wage increase of 3% or more depend on your ability to convince your boss that you deserve it. There may be many reasons your employer doesn't give you a raise that you consider adequate, and we could spend days discussing them.

Chief among them: Salary increases and inflation are not the same thing. They have different causes and effects. A massive rise in inflation like the one we saw in the wake of the pandemic can make companies cut back on salaries and hiring, if they fear that people are going to spend less and require fewer of their goods and services. Companies have an objective of increasing profits and revenues and keeping a lid on costs, and salaries are one of those costs. Your employer may think you're going to retire in a couple of years and decide there's little incentive in bumping up your pay. I don't agree with that logic, but it's a possibility. Or, in your manager's eyes, you may simply have reached the salary limit for your position.

Salary increases and the rise in the cost of goods and services are not the same thing. They have different causes and effects.

"Meeting expectations," while likely sufficient to keep your job, will not always be enough to result in a pay raise that matches or exceeds inflation. And pointing out that you are losing $1,000 a year in 401(k) contributions or creating a chart to show how what you regard as a less-than-fair salary is adversely impacting the 35 peak earning years used to calculate your Social Security benefits won't move the needle either. If you're unhappy at work, disgruntled or believe others are being favored, rightly or wrongly, that may also affect your performance.

You can pull out your soap box in the middle of your open-plan office and make a speech about how unfair it all is, but we'll likely never know the real reason behind the level of your raises. When you walk into your annual performance review, you should ideally be able to say how you contributed to the growth of the company, explain what unique gifts or skills you bring to it, what projects you have initiated and how you keep expanding your role and encouraging others to do the same. It should be an evidence-based discussion, with goals and objectives.

For now, you can continue to advocate for yourself at work or look at other jobs in a similar field that pay better, taking employee benefits like healthcare and 401(k) matching into account. (Switching jobs is not a guarantee of a higher salary, but it can be an effective way do increase your pay.) From a financial perspective, it's best to not look at what you don't have. Instead, look at your retirement income at 70, when you will get your full Social Security benefits plus 8% for every year you wait past your full retirement age, and then figure out how to spend the next chapter of your life.

Ask your manager what criteria are taken into account when management decides on individual pay raises.

I can understand why you're upset. I'm reminded of this study released by the Workforce Institute at UKG, which provides research and education on workplace issues. It concluded that 69% of workers said their manager had an impact on their mental health - the same percentage as said that about their spouse or partner and higher than those who said doctors (51%) or therapists (41%) affected their mental health. Your work is a big part of your life. Eight hours a day is no joke. And monetary reward is the biggest way to know you are valued.

It's also true that a gender wage gap persists in corporate America and is closing only at a glacial pace. Nearly three times as many working mothers as fathers quit their jobs to care for a family member, and a higher percentage turn down promotions. But inflation-indexed wage increases are not covered under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act. That legislation covers several protected classes: race, color, religion, sex and gender identity, national origin, age (for people 40 or over), disability and genetic information.

Here is my three-point plan for figuring out whether your salary is where it should be: 1. Ask your peers to give you a range for their own salaries. 2. Ask your manager what criteria are taken into account when management decides upon individual pay raises. 3. Have a private, respectful conversation with your manager, without rancor or using phrases like "it's not fair," and always come back to your own added value and what you bring to the company that is unique and has the most impact.

And in seven years, you will have achieved something millions of Americans dream of: freedom from office politics and the 9-to-5 grind.

Related: My therapist said he was in-network. He's not. Am I obliged to pay out-of-network rates for all prior sessions?

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

My daughter has $500K in med-school expenses. Can my wife and I afford to pay it off?

Can my husband contest his late brother's $600K will? He experienced oxygen deprivation due to COPD before he died.

I have early Alzheimer's and my husband has stage 4 kidney disease. We just inherited $50K. How can this help us?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  08-01-25 0919ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Roll Out the Magenta Welcome Mat: T-Mobile Completes UScellular Deal
data.description:
Roll Out the Magenta Welcome Mat: T-Mobile Completes UScellular Deal

Combined Company Will Deliver Improved Network Experiences, Low Prices and New Benefits to Millions More Customers Across the U.S.

T-Mobile (NASDAQ: TMUS) today announced it has closed its acquisition of UScellular’s wireless operations — a big win for U.S. mobile and broadband consumers across the country. More than four million UScellular customers, including businesses, will soon get access to blazing fast speeds on the nation’s best network and have the opportunity to unlock value, new perks and savings on a T-Mobile plan. Plus, hundreds of thousands of households in UScellular’s footprint that previously lacked access to high-speed connectivity will soon be eligible for T-Mobile’s popular in-home broadband service. And T-Mobile customers who live in, travel to, or pass through the UScellular footprint will benefit from enhanced network coverage, too.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250731989036/en/

T-Mobile announced it has closed its acquisition of UScellular’s wireless operations. More than four million UScellular customers, including businesses, will soon get access to blazing fast speeds on the nation’s best network and have the opportunity to unlock value, new perks and savings on a T-Mobile plan. Plus, hundreds of thousands of households in UScellular’s footprint that previously lacked access to high-speed connectivity will soon be eligible for T-Mobile’s popular in-home broadband service.

“Today is such an exciting one because we get to officially welcome UScellular customers to Team Magenta and we’re doing it with some pretty amazing Un-carrier gifts: America’s best network, value-packed benefits, and a best-in-class experience,” said Mike Sievert, CEO of T-Mobile. “We’re improving experiences for millions of UScellular and T-Mobile customers and adding more amazing employees to the T-Mobile family to help us do it.”

“For over 40 years, UScellular has been dedicated to the mission of connecting people to what matters most. The completion of our transaction with T-Mobile ensures that mission endures, as customers across our footprint will have an even better network experience,” said Laurent Therivel, former CEO of UScellular. “I am proud of the entire UScellular team and grateful for their contributions to our company and their support of our customers over the years. The completion of this transaction with T-Mobile is a win for customers and communities across the United States.”

Delivering More for Customers from Day One

  • Superior Network Experience, Seamless Transition. UScellular customers now on America’s best network will notice performance improvements from day one. With the companies’ combined footprint, both T-Mobile and UScellular customers will benefit from enhanced coverage. UScellular customers will move beyond limited roaming to a broader, more powerful experience — starting with reciprocal roaming and evolving into an integrated network experience in the coming months. And the transition will be seamless: nearly all of UScellular customer devices will continue to work on the T-Mobile network.
  • More Value, New Benefits, Low Prices. UScellular customers stay on their existing plans with no changes for now but as networks and systems are integrated, customers can choose to move to one of T-Mobile’s unlimited plans where they would unlock added value like streaming benefits (Netflix, AppleTV+, MLB.TV), free international roaming in over 215 countries and destinations, free in-flight Wi-Fi, built-in device upgrades, and more. Customers can also choose to level up to the new Experience plans with top-tier benefits like T-Satellite or select plans tailored for 55+ seniors, first responders and teachers. Starting August 7, customers will get a taste of what it’s like to be with the Un-carrier as they get access to customer-favorite dining and travel benefits. To come: every customer will get access to the Un-carrier’s award-winning Care support, more retail locations, expanded digital support and even more savings.
  • More Choice, Better Coverage. With T-Mobile’s best-in-class network, customers will have more choices than ever before, including T-Mobile’s home broadband and fixed wireless products. As the Un-carrier expands availability of these products, hundreds of thousands more households, including those in rural communities, will have a new option for reliable, high-speed internet they didn’t previously have — further closing the digital divide.

Transaction Summary

T-Mobile acquired substantially all of UScellular’s wireless operations, including its wireless customers and stores, as well as certain specified spectrum assets for an aggregate purchase price of approximately $4.3 billion after adjustments, consisting of $2.6 billion paid in cash and approximately $1.7 billion in debt to be assumed through an exchange offer made to UScellular’s debtholders, which is expected to close on August 5, 2025. In connection with this transaction, T-Mobile completed the Iowa Transactions1 for an additional payment of approximately $175 million in the aggregate, after adjustments. The UScellular brand will transition in phases to T-Mobile. Array Digital Infrastructure Inc. (formerly UScellular) will retain ownership of a significant portion of the company’s spectrum and towers, continuing operations as a separate infrastructure-focused entity. T-Mobile expects no impact from the acquisition to the company’s previous shareholder return authorization of up to $14 billion in 2025 and will provide an update on the financial impact of the transaction prior to or during its Q3 earnings call. All amounts are preliminary estimates and remain subject to customary purchase price and working capital adjustments following the closing.

Best network: Best mobile network in the U.S.based on analysis by Ookla® of Speedtest Intelligence® data 1H 2025. Ookla trademarks used under license and reprinted with permission.

Advisors

Cleary Gottlieb Steen & Hamilton LLP and DLA Piper LLP served as legal counsel to T-Mobile.

About T-Mobile US, Inc.

T-Mobile US, Inc. (NASDAQ: TMUS) is America’s supercharged Un-carrier, delivering an advanced 4G LTE and transformative nationwide 5G network that will offer reliable connectivity for all. T-Mobile’s customers benefit from its unmatched combination of value and quality, unwavering obsession with offering them the best possible service experience and undisputable drive for disruption that creates competition and innovation in wireless and beyond. Based in Bellevue, Wash., T-Mobile provides services through its subsidiaries and operates its flagship brands, T-Mobile, Metro by T-Mobile, and Mint Mobile. For more information please visit: https://www.t-mobile.com.

Cautionary Statement Regarding Forward-Looking Statements

This communication contains certain forward-looking statements concerning T-Mobile. All statements other than statements of fact, including information concerning future results, are forward-looking statements. These forward-looking statements are generally identified by the words “plan,” “anticipate,” “believe,” “estimate,” “expect,” “intend,” “may,” “could” or similar expressions. Such forward-looking statements include, but are not limited to, statements about the benefits of the acquisition of substantially all of UScellular’s wireless operations, including anticipated future financial and operating results and T-Mobile’s objectives, expectations and intentions. There are several factors which could cause actual plans and results to differ materially from those expressed or implied in forward-looking statements. Such factors include, but are not limited to, adverse economic, political or market conditions in the U.S. and international markets; negative effects of the acquisition on the market price of T-Mobile’s common stock and on T-Mobile’s operating results, including as a result of changes in key customer, supplier, employee or other business relationships; costs or difficulties related to the integration of UScellular’s operations and financial reporting and internal controls into T-Mobile; effects of changes in the regulatory environment in which T-Mobile operates; the risk of litigation or regulatory actions; and other risks and uncertainties detailed in T-Mobile’s Annual Report on Form 10-K for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, including in the sections thereof captioned “Risk Factors” and “Cautionary Statement Regarding Forward-Looking Statements,” as well as in its subsequent reports on Form 8-K and Form 10-Q, all of which are filed with the SEC and available at www.sec.gov and www.t-mobile.com. Forward-looking statements are based on current expectations and assumptions, which are subject to risks and uncertainties that may cause actual results to differ materially from those expressed in or implied by such forward-looking statements. Given these risks and uncertainties, persons reading this communication are cautioned not to place undue reliance on such forward-looking statements. T-Mobile assumes no obligation to update or revise the information contained in this communication (whether as a result of new information, future events or otherwise), except as required by applicable law. References to our and the SEC’s website are inactive textual references only. Information contained on our and the SEC’s website is not incorporated by reference in this communication and should not be considered to be a part of this communication.

1 On July 22, 2025, we entered into asset purchase agreements for the acquisition of substantially all of the wireless operations assets of each of Farmers Cellular Telephone Company, Inc., Iowa RSA No. 9 Limited Partnership, and Iowa RSA No. 12 Limited Partnership (collectively, the “Iowa Entities”) for aggregate cash consideration totaling approximately $175 million (collectively, the “Iowa Transactions”). The Iowa Transactions are expected to close concurrently with the UScellular Acquisition on August 1, 2025, at which time we expect to account for them as a business combination. Following the closing of the Iowa Transactions, the Iowa Entities will retain ownership of their towers and spectrum.

T-Mobile US, Inc. Media Relations
MediaRelations@t-mobile.com

T-Mobile Investor Relations
investor.relations@t-mobile.com
https://investor.t-mobile.com

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250731989036/en/

data.title: Newmont Gets Payment from Zijin Mining as Part of Sale of Ghana Gold Operation
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart 




  Newmont collected $100 million from Chinese miner Zijin Mining Group as part of the gold and copper producer's exit from a mine in Ghana. 



  Newmont said Friday that Ghana's parliament ratified the renewal of the Akyem East mining lease, triggering a payment by Zijin as part of the Denver-based company's deal to sell its Akyem operations in Ghana to the Chinese company. 



  Newmont said the payment brings after-cash proceeds from the sale of Akyem to about $770 million. 



  It now expects to generate $3.1 billion after tax from a 2025 divestment program, including $2.6 billion from selling assets and about $470 million offloading equity stakes in Greatland Resources and Discovery Silver. The money will be used to cut debt and return capital to shareholders, and to support Newmont's investment priorities, it said. 



  Newmont announced last October that it had reached a deal to sell Akyem, one of Ghana's largest gold operations, to Hong Kong-listed Zijin. The sale of the Akyem operation, along with Newmont's Porcupine operations in Canada, were finalized in April. 





  Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  August 01, 2025 09:08 ET (13:08 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
